title: UK DRS explained in 60 seconds
facts: The scheme launches on 1 October 2027.
A flat 20p refundable deposit applies to eligible containers.
Eligible containers are single-use PET plastic bottles and aluminium or steel cans from 150ml to 3 litres.
Consumers reclaim the deposit by returning an eligible empty container to an approved return point.

title: Why the UK needs DRS
facts: Around 25 billion single-use drinks containers are bought each year across England, Scotland and Northern Ireland.
An estimated 6.5 billion bottles and cans go to waste rather than being recycled each year.
DRS creates a cleaner, closed-loop material stream and reduces reliance on virgin material.

title: Bottles and cans are a major litter problem
facts: Drinks bottles and cans make up 55% of litter by volume according to Keep Britain Tidy.
International schemes have reported major reductions in bottle and can litter after DRS introduction.

title: The 90% collection ambition
facts: Exchange for Change is designing the scheme to achieve a 90% collection rate within three years of launch.
Higher return rates support cleaner material streams and bottle-to-bottle or can-to-can recycling.

title: Retailer obligations from 1 October 2027
facts: Retailers selling in-scope drinks must charge the 20p deposit at the point of sale.
Groceries retailers must register and operate a manual or automated return point unless exempt.
Return point operators must repay deposits, store returned containers and display customer information.

title: Which containers are in scope?
facts: Single-use PET plastic bottles and aluminium or steel cans are in scope.
Container sizes run from 150ml to 3 litres.
Glass and HDPE milk bottles are not in scope for the Exchange for Change scheme.

title: Does your store need a return point?
facts: Urban groceries retailers under 100m² are automatically exempt from hosting a return point.
Urban retailers from 100m² to 199m² and rural retailers under 200m² may apply for a size-based exemption.
Other exemptions may cover proximity, listed buildings, site access or utility limitations.
Exempt retailers must still charge the deposit when selling in-scope drinks.

title: £6,000 support for qualifying small retailers
facts: Exchange for Change has announced a £60 million grant fund for up to 10,000 qualifying small independent retailers.
Eligible sites can receive £6,000 in three annual payments of £2,000 after installing an RVM.
Detailed eligibility and application guidance is due from Exchange for Change.

title: Return handling fees: what retailers can earn
facts: Manual return points receive 3p per eligible container.
Automated return points receive 5p per container for up to 225,000 annual returns.
Automated returns above 225,000 annually receive 1.3p per container.
The fee is intended to help cover equipment, staffing, training, floor space and storage costs.

title: Manual return point vs RVM
facts: At a manual return point, staff check containers, issue refunds, bag and store returns.
An RVM scans and validates containers, issues a redeemable voucher and compacts accepted material for storage.
The right choice depends on store footprint, staff capacity and expected return volume.

title: What retailers should ask an RVM supplier
facts: Check the footprint against available store space.
Match throughput and storage capacity to expected return volumes.
Require evidence that the machine meets Exchange for Change specifications and certification requirements.
Ask about service response, remote monitoring, data security, accessibility and deposit repayment options.

title: How a compliant RVM recognises a container
facts: The minimum recognition combination includes barcode, basic shape, weight and metal detection.
The machine must identify registered products from the Exchange for Change Article List.
RVMs must reject non-empty or ineligible containers and support anti-fraud controls.

title: The data and connectivity behind every return
facts: RVMs must integrate with the scheme Article List and report transaction data.
Transaction data must be protected with encryption and access controls.
Offline transactions must be stored and forwarded when connectivity returns.
RVM suppliers will need to enter a data processing agreement with Exchange for Change before installation.

title: Accessible returns by design
facts: RVM installations should comply with disability access requirements, including the Equality Act 2010.
The consumer interface should be clear, consistent and easy to use.
The machine must provide an approved way to repay the deposit, with paper vouchers as a minimum option.

title: Retailer readiness timeline
facts: Start by measuring store space, expected return volume and staffing capacity.
Compare manual return and automated RVM operating models.
Follow Exchange for Change guidance on registration, exemptions, grants and accredited suppliers.
Complete installation, staff training and customer communication before 1 October 2027.

title: The customer return journey
facts: Buy an eligible drink and pay the 20p deposit.
Keep the empty container identifiable and return it to an approved return point.
Receive the deposit refund through the approved repayment method.

title: From bottle to bottle. From can to can.
facts: DRS creates a separate, cleaner collection stream for PET plastic and metals.
Cleaner material can improve recyclate quality and support closed-loop recycling.
Reusing material reduces demand for virgin resources.

title: Building a convenient return network
facts: Groceries retailers are intended to form the primary return-point network.
A mix of manual and automated return points can serve different store formats and communities.
Exemptions depend on maintaining sufficient local access to return points.

title: Who runs the scheme?
facts: Exchange for Change is the not-for-profit Deposit Management Organisation for England, Scotland and Northern Ireland.
It is responsible for designing and delivering the scheme infrastructure and operating rules.
Producers, retailers and the wider supply chain have responsibilities under the scheme.

title: Wales: a separate DRS pathway
facts: Wales is developing a separate scheme intended to launch on 1 October 2027.
Its scope includes PET, aluminium, steel and glass containers from 150ml to 3 litres.
Glass is planned to carry a zero-pence deposit during a transition period to 30 September 2031.
Do not combine Welsh glass rules with Exchange for Change messaging for England, Scotland and Northern Ireland.

title: DRS within the wider packaging reform programme
facts: DRS sits alongside packaging Extended Producer Responsibility and simpler recycling reforms.
The Government estimates the combined packaging reforms could stimulate £10 billion of recycling investment over ten years and support up to 21,000 jobs in England.
These investment and jobs figures must not be attributed to DRS alone.

title: Zero-capex RVM partnership for retailers
facts: ReteaRn's proposed partnership model is designed around zero upfront capex for the retailer.
ReteaRn owns and operates the machine, with monitoring and maintenance support.
Retailers can focus on compliance and customer experience while receiving the applicable return handling fee.

title: Reklaim Ace: compact AI-powered RVM
facts: The Reklaim Ace brochure states a 10.9 sq ft footprint, 900L storage and capacity for more than 1,500 returns per day.
The product is presented as an indoor or rain-proof outdoor, fully unsupervised RVM with remote monitoring.
All UK deployment claims remain subject to Exchange for Change certification and final accreditation.

title: How to compare RVM suppliers
scope: Internal sales enablement or neutral public education
facts: Compare accredited compliance, store footprint, throughput, storage, uptime, service coverage, integration and total operating model.
Established suppliers position around different strengths, including global installed base, modular portfolios, compact machines and high-volume systems.
Public creatives should use a neutral supplier checklist rather than unverified competitor performance claims.
